text: Hello, I'm sharing another laparoscopic appendectomy for appendicitis. The difference in this case is that there was a large phlegmon right on the base of the cecum. And the patient's story was that they had over a week of pain and fevers. They were reluctant to come into the emergency room, which is why it got this bad. The finding on CAT scan was read as phlegmon. I know there's a lot of differences in the way you can manage patients like this. You could choose to give antibiotics and wait for things to cool down, or you can go in for surgery. Obviously, a lot of people have concerns that with surgery, there could be conversion to open or the need for an ileocolic resection. I'm very comfortable with these and just going to the operating room. And that's what I chose for this patient.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the omentum to expose the appendix, revealing the localized phlegmon at the base of the cecum. The yellow anti-mesenteric fat on the terminal ileum is visible above the ligature. The appendix appears folded, and the surgeon mobilizes the terminal ileum and cecum to improve access and angles for dissection, ensuring the ureter is checked for safety.

The surgeon carefully retracts the omentum from the appendix, revealing the localized phlegmon at the base of the cecum. The yellow anti-mesenteric fat on the terminal ileum is visible above the ligature.

The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix, revealing its curlicue nature as it unfolds. Due to difficulty in elevating the appendix, the surgeon mobilizes the terminal ileum and cecum, ensuring the ureter is checked beforehand. This maneuver increases the mobility of the cecum and appendix, allowing for better angles for dissection.

The surgeon identifies the wall of the cecum and performs blunt dissection to increase mobility. The mesoappendix is located above the terminal ileal fat, and the surgeon uses a surgical instrument to dissect through the mesoappendix, revealing pus drainage from the abscess in the mesentery. The dissection creates a plane between the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum and the mesoappendix, allowing further dissection through the mesentery.

The surgeon identifies the wall of the cecum and performs blunt dissection to increase mobility. The mesoappendix is located above the terminal ileal fat, and the surgeon prepares to dissect through it, ensuring the appendix's folded position is understood.

The surgeon divides the base of the appendix and enters the phlegmon, with visible pus draining from the mesentery to the appendix. The dissection progresses between the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum and the mesoappendix, clearing the way to continue cutting through the mesentery.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the mesoappendix, revealing the base of the appendix, which appears thinned out. The surgeon uses a ligature to transect the remaining mesoappendix, lifting it away from the lateral aspect of the phlegmon. A stapler is prepared for use to secure the area.

The surgeon manipulates the mesoappendix, revealing the thinned-out base of the appendix. A ligature is used to grasp the tissue, and the surgeon considers using a stapler to transect the mesoappendix, followed by over-sewing the area. The lateral aspect of the phlegmon is visible, and the surgeon evaluates the best approach for securing the tissue.

The surgeon prepares to over-sew the stump of the appendix using a 2-0 Vicryl suture, taking large bites to bunch the tissue together. A long stitch is used for multiple sutures, with figure-of-eight patterns applied to secure the area.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture to over-sew the stump of the appendix, taking large bites of tissue to secure the area. The long stitch is used to ensure multiple sutures can be placed, effectively bunching up the surrounding tissue.

I do not leave drains for appendixes like this and in fact I very rarely leave drains even for perforated appendicitis. This is the type of patient that I do leave on antibiotics for about three days. The vast majority of my appendicitis cases I will send home from the recovery room. This was a patient that I left in house for about twenty three hours for IV antibiotics prior to discharge. Given the inflammatory markers for her were so high with a white count over twenty seven thousand and fevers to one hundred and three. This is my second suture.
The surgeon performs an omentopexy by taking a bite of the omentum and securing it to the lateral border of the appendix stump, ensuring it remains in place to seal the area.
The surgeon performs an omentopexy by taking a bite of the omentum and securing it to the lateral border of the appendix stump, ensuring it remains in place to seal the area.

I have a little suture management issue here. This patient did do well. I think early in my career, when I've had cases like this with that stump of the appendix, I probably left a drain. I have not had any concerns. I've not had any stump leaks. I've not had any abscesses. So I'm very happy with leaving things as they are. And this is another patient who did well, and that's just my approach. I hope you enjoyed.
